key_insight: A neural operator can act as a learned preconditioner for Helmholtz systems, and training on a mixed source/velocity dataset substantially improves robustness and convergence in transcranial ultrasound simulations.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical path to accelerate large-scale, patient-specific wave solvers that are otherwise difficult for standard Krylov methods to handle.
method: Train a neural operator on coarse-grid Helmholtz solves using physics-informed relative residual loss.
Use mixed datasets combining randomized sources, idealized skull velocities, and noise to improve generalization.
Embed the learned operator as a nonlinear preconditioner inside FGMRES.
evidence_signal: The method solved transcranial ultrasound cases on grids 64× larger than training and outperformed classical GMRES and the learned optimizer, which did not converge within similar budgets.
watch_item: Performance likely depends on dataset design and coarse-to-fine transfer, so robustness should be rechecked when velocity fields or discretizations change substantially.
paper_title: Neural operator preconditioning from mixed dataset for the Helmholtz equations: Application to transcranial ultrasound
